course_description: Economics of the growth strategies of modern corporations, including vertical integration, diversification and multinational enterprise. Economic underpinnings of marketing management, including economics of retailing and Internet businesses.

requirements.incompatible: ID226 or EC265 or ECON2400 or 2700
requirements.assumed_background: Students are expected to have an understanding of first level principles of microeconomics. The mathematical requirements of this course are modest. Basic arithmetic and calculus as well as an ability to learn to understand and manipulate simple graphs are required.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Additional Timetable Information
Tutorials commence in Week 2.

Students are required to sign-on to a tutorial via mySI-net. Sign-on commences on Friday 20th July and closes Friday 24th August .

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: The purpose of ECON2410 is to provide students with an economic framework that can be used to analyse strategic situations and problems faced by modern businesses and corporations. The economic principles examined in the course also underpin thinking in management disciplines such as marketing and strategic management. At the completion of the course students should be able to identify the appropriate economic tools to use to analyse a range of business problems.

learning_outcomes.description: Use microeconomic theory to analyse a range of strategic problems facing modern business enterprises.
Appreciate the insights and limits of economic theory in operating a business.
Have an understanding through economics of why business follows particular practices in different industries.
Have the ability to define and analyse problems based on modelling frameworks and an understanding of relevant data and institutional detail.

assessment_details.title: Mid-semester exam
assessment_details.task_description: Formal examination to be held in class in Teaching Week 8 on Monday 3rd September, 2012. The mid-semester examination will be 90 minutes long with an additional 5 minutes for perusal. The exam will cover material from the first FIVE (5) lectures. It will consist of multiple choice and short-answer questions.

assessment_details.title: Final Exam
assessment_details.task_description: The final examination will be a 120 minute examination with an additional 10 minutes for perusal. The examination will consist of material dealt with from ALL of the lectures and tutorials from throughout the semester. Particular emphasis will be placed on the topics that have not been already examined in the mid-semester examination. It will consist of multiple choice and short-answer questions. The final exam will be held during the examination period at a time and place set by the Examinations Section.

learning_resources.required_resources:
The lectures and tutorial sessions are based on the textbook listed below, but will cover issues in further depth. Copies of the textbook are available in the library.

Besanko, D., Dranove, D., Shanley, M. and Schaefer, S. (2010) Economics of Strategy , 5th Ed., New Jersey: Jon Wiley & Sons. [HD30.28 .E25 2010].

The 4th edition (2007) of this textbook may be available second hand. Most of the revisions in the new edition have been minor, even though the selected chapters have been reordered and re-numbered.
learning_resources.recommended_resources:
Students are expected to have an understanding of first level principles of microeconomics. However, Chapter 1 of the textbook offers a compact revision of microeconomic principles which can be used as a refresher course and/or referred back to when reading other chapters. If more detailed explanations of microeconomic theories are required, students are recommended to read:

Pindyck, R. S. and Rubinfeld, D. L. (2009) Microeconomics , 7th Ed., New Jersey: Pearson Prentice-Hall. [SS&H HB172 .P53 2009]

or an equivalent intermediate microeconomics textbook. There are a large number of texts available, but please ensure they are recent editions and cover course materials.
learning_resources.university_learning_resources:
Access to required and recommended resources, plus past central exam papers, is available at the UQ Library website ( http://library.uq.edu.au/search/r?SEARCH=ECON2410 ).

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myServices website ( https://student.my.uq.edu.au/ ).
learning_resources.school_learning_resources:
From Semester 1, 2011 The School of Economics has adopted the new University Calculator Scheme. For assessment in this course students will be permitted to use Casio FX82 series (82 with any letters) or university approved (labelled) calculators . For further details on approved calculators http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/exam-calculators .

Calculator requirements may differ for other university courses and students should check each course profile for the specific requirements.

PLEASE NOTE: It is the students responsibility to ensure they have the correct calculator prior to assessment in each of their courses. Students without the correct calculator in a School of Economics exam may have their calculator confiscated.

Guidelines for correct referencing techniques can be found in the School of Economics publication Guide for Assignment Presentation , which can be accessed on the web site www.uq.edu.au/economics
learning_resources.other_learning_resources:
It is recommended that students monitor the business press for topics that are relevant applications of the course concepts. The UQ Library provides access to a large number of national and international newspapers (e.g., AFR, The Australian, The Guardian, Financial Times, etc.). The UQ Library also has a large collection of journals, industry reports, and other electronic resources, and students are strongly encouraged to make full use of these resources when studying for this course.

Previous 2. Aims

Next 4. Activities

learning_activities.topic: Week 1: Intro.& Economic Foundations of Strategy (Lecture): Introduction to the concept of strategy and basic economic principles. Readings/Ref: Besanko et al. (Chapters 1 & 2)

Week 2: Economics of Scale and Scope (Lecture): The growth of firms depends critically on the availability of economics of scale and/or scope. We examine how this impacts upon firm strategy and competitive outcomes. Readings/Ref: Besanko et al. (Chapter 2)

Week 3: The Vertical Boundaries of the Firm (Lecture): Firms are connected to suppliers and to customers in vertical chains. We examine why it is sometimes rational for a firm to vertically integrate and sometimes not. Readings/Ref: Besanko et al. (Chapter 5)

Week 4: Organising Vertical Boundaries (Lecture): We examine different characteristics of industries, firms and transaction arrangements that determine whether vertical integration is worthwhile and whether there are other options. Readings/Ref: Besanko et al. (Chapter 6)

Week 5: Competitors and Competition (Lecture): We discuss why competition in markets is so important and how competition works in different kinds of market structures. Readings/Ref: Besanko et al. (Chapter 8)

Week 6: Strategic Commitment (Lecture): Investments in productive structures are, to a significant degree, irreversible. This commitment brings with it strategic considerations that the firm must deal with. Readings/Ref: Besanko et al. (Chapter 9)
Week 7: MID-SEMESTER EXAM (Test): Mid-Semester Examination (see assessment section)

Week 8: The Dynamics of Pricing Rivalry (Lecture): The structure of an industry is a determinant of whether firms set prices in a rivalrous or cooperative manner. And, as an industry evolves, this can change. Variations in product quality are also important in pricing decisions. Readings/Ref: Besanko et al. (Chapter 10)

Week 9: Entry & Exit (Lecture): Economic evolution and associated productivity growth occur because of firm turnover, induced by innovation and competition. Here, we consider the factors that encourage or deter entry and exit. Readings/Ref: Besanko et al. (Chapter 11)

Week 11:Strategic Positioning for Competitive Adv. (Lecture): Competitive advantage comes from superior profitability, but there are various ways that this can be achieved so the firm must make choices. This involves an understanding of product demand characteristics and the cost reduction possibilities that are offered by increasing the scale of production and engaging in innovation. Readings/Ref: Besanko et al. (Chapter 13)

Week 12: Sustaining Competitive Advantage (Lecture): A competitive advantage may be short-lived if other firms can easily replicate a production plan. Sustainability comes from preserving property rights in products and having a resource base that is unique and difficult to replicate. These considerations have important implications for the long term strategy of the firm. Readings/Ref: Besanko et al. (Chapter 14)

Week 13: Innovation, Evolution and the Environment (Lecture): Strategic decision-making leads to successes and failures. The successful come to dominate but they are ultimately challenged by new, innovative firms. We can look at this in 'evolutionary' terms as a process of 'creative destruction'. Thus, we can understand why capitalism exhibits ongoing productivity growth and increased diversity in the products and services on offer. Readings/Ref: Besanko et al. (Chapter 15)

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the Universitys assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity
It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism
The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

Medical grounds: Applications for extension on medical grounds shall be made by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and supporting documentation at the location outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course concerned.

Exceptional circumstances: Applications for extension on the grounds of exceptional circumstances shall be made to the relevant Course Coordinator/Program Director by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and a personal statement outlining the grounds for the application at the location and by the due date outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course in which this application is made. If the exceptional circumstances are such that the student cannot reasonably be expected to have complied with these conditions, a case should be made as to why these conditions could not be met.

Outcome of application: Students will be advised of the outcome of their application via their student email.

Feedback on Assessment
Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.02-assessment )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

School of Economics
Assessment Guidelines

School of Economics Assessment Guidelines

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses
Students on a placement course also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-placement-courses ) for detailed information.

Working with Children
Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25004 ) to find out how to apply for a blue card.

Students with a Disability
Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety
Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Next Summary
course_introduction:
Managers in modern businesses face a complex array of strategic decisions; any one of which may affect the viability of their organisation. In Economics of Business Strategy analytical tools are used to examine a range of common problems faced by senior managers, such as pricing decisions, which products to develop, and whether the firm should diversify its product range. While such problems can be considered from other perspectives, such as marketing, the economic perspective, with its focus on optimisation, provides a useful analytical framework.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

New staffing arrangements apply in 2012. Changes have been made in some areas of course content, and in assessment arrangements. Student feedback is not available at this time. We look forward to receiving your responses at the end of the semester.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY

B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .

D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D3 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions.

E2 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .

other_learning_activities_information:
Both lectures and tutorials are directly relevant to the assessment process and students should attend and participate in both sets of activities. Tutorial attendances will not be compulsory and no marks will be allocated for attendance. Nevertheless, attending tutorials will give students an 'edge' during examinations as questions are explained in greater detail compared to answers posted on Blackboard. Students are expected to come to tutorials prepared , having already done the necessary reading and exercises.

Some students may find lectures to be intense, and it will not be unusual to go through a substantial amount of material in one session. Ideally, students should try and ensure that they briefly review the relevant part of the text prior to attending the lectures and read in detail after the lecture. Otherwise, students might find it difficult to cope with the material presented.

ECON2410 is a #2 unit course. UQ's expectation is that a #2 unit course requires a time allocation of, on average, 10 hours per week. Lectures and tutorials only account for 3 hours per week. Therefore, if you are attending all of these contact sessions, it is expected that you will be spending a further 7 hours per week in self-directed learning, particularly if you are aiming to achieve a distinction grade in the course . This could include attending consultation sessions, reading the textbook, attempting practice questions at the end of each textbook chapter etc. Please give serious consideration as to whether you are willing and able to make this time commitment before enrolling. Experience shows that students who do not put in this basic time allocation tend to struggle.

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

Awarded for an overall mark in the range 0-19.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

Awarded for an overall mark in the range 20-44.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

Awarded for an overall mark in the range 45-49.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Awarded for an overall mark in the range 50-64.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Awarded for an overall mark in the range 65-74.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

Awarded for an overall mark in the range 75-84.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

Awarded for an overall mark of 85.00 percent and above.

Other Requirements & Comments:

As per School of Economics policy, percentage marks will NOT be rounded (e.g. 84.99 will remain as 84.99 and the student is awarded a 6).
other_assessment_information:
The School of Economics is committed to reducing the incidence of plagiarism. Further information on plagiarism and how to avoid an allegation of plagiarism is available under Section 6. Policies and Guidelines - 6.1. Assessment Related Policies & Guidelines of this Course Profile.

At the end of the semester, a student's best score among the following three weighting schemes will be used by the Course Coordinator to allocate the student's overall grade for the course .

Scheme 1: Mid-Semester Exam 40%, Final Exam 60%
Scheme 2: Mid-Semester Exam 30%, Final Exam 70%
Scheme 3: Mid-Semester Exam 50%, Final Exam 50%

DEFERRED EXAMINATIONS

If you are unable to attend a scheduled exam (e.g. due to illness or other unavoidable circumstances which can be evidenced - e.g. medical certificate), you may be eligible to sit a deferred examination at a later time.

Information about deferred examinations can be found on the web at http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2995 Please read this information carefully.

All applications for deferred exams must be submitted within five (5) days of the original examination.

Mid-semester Examination (SCHOOL exam) - submit your application to the Course Coordinator with appropriate documentation. Do not submit to the Faculty Collaborative Research Centre or the Student Centre.

End of Semester Examination (CENTRAL exam) - submit your application to the Student Centre, Level 1, J.D. Story Building with appropriate documentation.

SUPPLEMENTARY EXAMINATIONS

If you fail a course (generally with a grade of three), you may be eligible to sit a supplementary exam. You can find information about supplementary examinations at: http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2954 . Read this information very carefully to see if you may be eligible to apply for a supplementary examination. Also see General Award Rules, Section 1A.17.3, and your program rules.

Applications for supplementary exams must be lodged online via mySI-net, within 5 days of the official release of results.

Important Information regarding Supplementary Examinations for courses within the Faculty of Business, Economics & Law:

· Supplementary Examinations are weighted at 100% (i.e. progressive assessment does not count) and therefore ALL modules and topics are assessable.

· Results for Supplementary Examinations cannot be graded higher than a four (4).

· Once approved, applications cannot be rescinded.

· Students are advised to take the Deferred & Supplementary Exam period into account when making post-examination plans (12-14 December).
